g.data-narration: Environmental legislation includes laws protecting air, water, soil, and biodiversity. These regulations govern human activities impacting our natural environment.
g.text: Collection of laws, regulations, and policies designed to
protect the environment and natural resources

g.data-narration: India's Constitution provides the foundation for environmental protection through Article 48A and Article 51A, establishing both state responsibilities and citizen duties.
India's environmental laws evolved chronologically: Water Act in 1974, Air Act in 1981, and the comprehensive Environment Protection Act in 1986.
The Environment Protection Act 1986 is India's most comprehensive environmental law, empowering the Central Government to set standards for environmental pollutants.
g.text: • Imprisonment up to 5 years for violations

g.data-narration: The Water Act of 1974 established CPCB and SPCBs, prohibiting pollutant discharge into water bodies beyond specified limits. Violations can result in 3-year imprisonment.
g.text: No discharge of pollutants beyond limits

g.data-narration: The Air Act 1981 empowers pollution control boards to enforce air quality standards, regulate industrial emissions, and address vehicular pollution.
The E-Waste Rules 2022 introduced EPR, requiring manufacturers to collect and recycle e-waste. Collection targets start at 10% and increase to 70% over 7 years.
EPR means producers bear responsibility for collecting and recycling their products' end-of-life. They must register on the portal and file annual returns by April 30th.
India is party to key international agreements: Paris Agreement (limit temperature rise), Montreal Protocol (ozone protection), and Convention on Biological Diversity.
Key regulatory bodies include MoEFCC (policy), CPCB (national coordination), SPCBs (state implementation), and NGT (environmental justice tribunal).
The National Green Tribunal, established in 2010, provides specialized and expedited justice for environmental cases, awarding compensation for environmental damage.
g.text: • Established 2010 | Jurisdiction over all environmental laws | Can award compensation for environmental damage

g.data-narration: For a data center, companies must obtain SPCB consent, install emission controls, monitor air quality, and file annual returns under the Air Act 1981.
Computer manufacturers must register on EPR portal, calculate collection targets, establish collection mechanisms, and file returns by April 30th.
g.text: Example: E-Waste Compliance for Computer Manufacturers

g.data-narration: Before establishing an IT park, developers must submit EIA report, conduct public hearings, get clearance from experts, and implement environmental management plans.
Remember: Water Act (1974), Air Act (1981), Environment Protection Act (1986) form the backbone. Key definitions and EPR are crucial for exams.
Know regulatory body roles, penalties, and India's international commitments under Paris Agreement (33-35% emission intensity reduction by 2030).
Engineering professionals must ensure environmental compliance in their careers. Sustainable practices and e-waste management are critical responsibilities for CSE engineers.
g.text: As future engineers, ensure environmental compliance and adopt sustainable practices
E-waste management is a critical responsibility for CSE professionals
